<findings>

[observation] [info] High cohesion (0.87) — files are tightly interconnected
[observation] [warning] 3 outbound imports into web-viewer from this server-side zone invert the expected dependency direction; trace these imports to determine whether a shared type or web-shared abstraction would eliminate the upward dependency.
[observation] [info] Cohesion of 0.87 across usage tracking, scheduler registration, and cleanup services confirms strong thematic grouping — this zone has a clear single responsibility.
[observation] [info] shared-types.ts and task-usage.ts as the only entry points provide a minimal, auditable public API surface — new server utilities added here should export through these two files rather than adding new entry points.
[relationship] [critical] server-usage-scheduler imports from web-viewer (3 imports) despite web-viewer being a higher-layer UI hub — server-side services must not depend on viewer code; these imports likely pull in types that should live in web-shared or a dedicated server-types module
[anti-pattern] [info] The callback-injection decoupling pattern used by server-usage-scheduler (injecting broadcast, loadPRD, collectAllIds at startup) is not documented in CLAUDE.md or in gateway module documentation. Without documentation, future contributors may bypass the injection seam and introduce direct viewer imports to restore 'simpler' access, undoing the decoupling. The pattern should be captured in the gateway module table as a named exemption: 'scheduler callbacks — injected at startup, not imported.'
[suggestion] [warning] Define a named TypeScript interface (e.g., SchedulerCallbacks) for the injected broadcast, loadPRD, and collectAllIds parameters. Without an explicit interface, the callback contract is distributed across call sites in start.ts and parameter declarations in register-scheduler.ts — a refactor of either side can silently break the injection seam without triggering a type error.
[suggestion] [info] The 1:1 test-to-production file ratio achieved by this zone should be documented as the target coverage standard for new server-side service zones in the web package. The ratio is a direct consequence of the callback-injection pattern eliminating viewer-side test dependencies — adopting the pattern in new server zones would propagate both the decoupling benefit and the coverage ratio.

</findings>

<insights>

- High cohesion (0.87) — files are tightly interconnected
- Strong cohesion (0.87) across 15 files (including tests) shows that incremental usage tracking, scheduler registration, and cleanup are correctly co-located as a single operational concern.
- The 3 imports from web-server into web-viewer represent an architectural inversion — scheduler and usage services should depend on shared types, not on viewer-layer modules.
- Entry points (shared-types.ts and task-usage.ts) form a clean two-surface public API appropriate for a focused service zone.
- 3 outbound imports into web-viewer from this server-side zone invert the expected dependency direction; trace these imports to determine whether a shared type or web-shared abstraction would eliminate the upward dependency.
- Cohesion of 0.87 across usage tracking, scheduler registration, and cleanup services confirms strong thematic grouping — this zone has a clear single responsibility.
- shared-types.ts and task-usage.ts as the only entry points provide a minimal, auditable public API surface — new server utilities added here should export through these two files rather than adding new entry points.
- The 3 outbound imports from server-usage-scheduler into web-viewer traverse two architectural boundaries simultaneously (server→viewer and usage-service→hub) — each import is doubly incorrect by direction
- server-usage-scheduler imports from web-viewer (3 imports) despite web-viewer being a higher-layer UI hub — server-side services must not depend on viewer code; these imports likely pull in types that should live in web-shared or a dedicated server-types module
- The zone achieves runtime decoupling from web-viewer not through the gateway pattern but through constructor-injected callbacks (broadcast, loadPRD, collectAllIds) supplied at startup by start.ts. This is a stronger isolation technique than gateway imports: the zone has zero static module imports from web-viewer, making it fully unit-testable without any viewer-side dependencies.
- The previous cross-zone insight flagging 'server-usage-scheduler → web-viewer: 3 imports' describes a resolved or stale state — current code inspection finds zero direct imports from this zone into web-viewer. The cross-zone edge may have been recorded before the callback-injection refactor landed.
- The callback-injection decoupling pattern used by server-usage-scheduler (injecting broadcast, loadPRD, collectAllIds at startup) is not documented in CLAUDE.md or in gateway module documentation. Without documentation, future contributors may bypass the injection seam and introduce direct viewer imports to restore 'simpler' access, undoing the decoupling. The pattern should be captured in the gateway module table as a named exemption: 'scheduler callbacks — injected at startup, not imported.'
- server-usage-scheduler has 7 test files for 8 production files — a near 1:1 test-to-production coverage ratio, the healthiest in the web package. This ratio reflects the testability benefits of the callback-injection decoupling pattern: injecting behavior at startup makes each service independently unit-testable without viewer-side dependencies. The pattern and its testability benefit should be co-documented as a named design pattern in CLAUDE.md.
- The callback-injection seam (broadcast, loadPRD, collectAllIds injected at startup by start.ts) is structurally invisible to import-graph analysis — the zone appears fully decoupled in zone metrics but has runtime behavioral coupling to caller-supplied implementations. This creates a class of 'dark coupling' not capturable by static zone analysis: the callbacks form an implicit interface contract that must be maintained across start.ts and register-scheduler.ts simultaneously. A TypeScript interface type for the injected callbacks would make this implicit contract explicit and type-checked.
- Define a named TypeScript interface (e.g., SchedulerCallbacks) for the injected broadcast, loadPRD, and collectAllIds parameters. Without an explicit interface, the callback contract is distributed across call sites in start.ts and parameter declarations in register-scheduler.ts — a refactor of either side can silently break the injection seam without triggering a type error.
- The 1:1 test-to-production file ratio achieved by this zone should be documented as the target coverage standard for new server-side service zones in the web package. The ratio is a direct consequence of the callback-injection pattern eliminating viewer-side test dependencies — adopting the pattern in new server zones would propagate both the decoupling benefit and the coverage ratio.
- [call graph] 219 internal calls, 9 outgoing, 88 incoming (cohesion: 0.96, coupling: 0.04)

</insights>
